title: Sharing a Studio: Two Designers Negotiate
summary: Two freelance designers sharing a small studio try to agree on how to split rent, hours and clients fairly, after months of small frustrations.

body.speaker: Sam
body.text: Andre, can we talk properly about the studio? I've been meaning to bring this up for weeks, and I think if we don't, it's going to start affecting our work.

body.speaker: Andre
body.text: Of course. I've felt something was off too, honestly. Tell me what's on your mind.

body.speaker: Sam
body.text: Okay. The rent split. We agreed fifty-fifty when we moved in, but I've been here in the evenings and weekends much more than you have. It's started to feel uneven.

body.speaker: Andre
body.text: That's a fair point. I hadn't realised how often you were here outside normal hours. Would you prefer a hours-based split, or something simpler?

body.speaker: Sam
body.text: I'd prefer something simpler, otherwise we'll spend every month arguing about timesheets. What if you paid forty and I paid sixty? That feels closer to actual usage.

body.speaker: Andre
body.text: I could live with that, as long as it covers everything — rent, utilities, the printer ink. I don't want to be nickel-and-dimed on top.

body.speaker: Sam
body.text: Agreed. Sixty-forty covers all shared costs. The only thing we'd still split equally is internet, because we both use it whether we're here or not.

body.speaker: Andre
body.text: Sounds reasonable. The bigger thing for me, honestly, is client space. When you have a client meeting, I can't really work. I lose half a day every time.

body.speaker: Sam
body.text: I know, and I'm sorry. I should have asked sooner. What if we book the studio for client meetings — even just a shared calendar? You'd get warning, and I'd be more disciplined.

body.speaker: Andre
body.text: That would help a lot. And if you have a meeting in the morning, I could just work from a café until lunchtime. It's the surprise that kills my focus, not the meeting itself.

body.speaker: Sam
body.text: Done. I'll put a calendar on the door tonight. Anything from your side that's been bothering you?

body.speaker: Andre
body.text: Honestly? The music. I love that you play it, but some days I need silence to think, and I find it hard to ask you to turn it off without sounding rude.

body.speaker: Sam
body.text: Oh, I had no idea. You should have said. From now on, just put on your headphones — that can be the signal that you need quiet. I'll get the message immediately.

body.speaker: Andre
body.text: That's much easier than having to say it out loud every time. Thank you.

body.speaker: Sam
body.text: One more thing while we're being honest. The pitch we lost last month — I felt you blamed me a little, even though we'd both worked on it.

body.speaker: Andre
body.text: I didn't mean to. If I came across that way, I apologise. I was disappointed in myself more than anything, but I should have been clearer about that.

body.speaker: Sam
body.text: Thanks for saying that. I think we needed this conversation months ago. The studio works because we trust each other, and small things were starting to eat into that.

body.speaker: Andre
body.text: They were. Let's agree to do this every couple of months — a proper check-in, not just over coffee. Better to fix small things than to wait until one of us wants to leave.

body.speaker: Sam
body.text: Agreed. I'd much rather negotiate with you over rent than spend six months looking for a new studio mate.

body.speaker: Andre
body.text: Same here. Right — calendar on the door, headphones for silence, sixty-forty on the rent. Anything I've forgotten?

body.speaker: Sam
body.text: Just one thing. If either of us lands a big client next year, we should talk about whether we expand or stay this size. I don't want to assume.

body.speaker: Andre
body.text: Good thinking. Let's add that to the next check-in. For now, I think we've earned a coffee — and a quiet hour to actually get some work done.

annotations.meaningVi: đề cập, nêu vấn đề ra
annotations.exampleEn: I've been meaning to bring this up at the meeting.

annotations.meaningVi: có gì đó không ổn, không bình thường
annotations.exampleEn: I could tell something was off the moment I walked in.
We need to discuss the rent split before signing the lease.

annotations.meaningVi: ngoài giờ làm việc bình thường
annotations.exampleEn: She often works outside normal hours to meet deadlines.

annotations.meaningVi: cách chia dựa trên số giờ sử dụng
annotations.exampleEn: An hours-based split is fairer but requires careful tracking.

annotations.meaningVi: bị tính từng đồng lẻ, bị tính phí lặt vặt
annotations.exampleEn: I hate being nickel-and-dimed for every small extra at the hotel.
We use a shared calendar so no one books the meeting room twice.
I need to be more disciplined about how I plan my week.

annotations.meaningVi: phá hỏng sự tập trung của tôi
annotations.exampleEn: An unexpected phone call kills my focus for the rest of the morning.

annotations.meaningVi: mà không tỏ ra thô lỗ
annotations.exampleEn: It's hard to say no without sounding rude.
I had no idea you were uncomfortable with the noise.
If she stops replying, you should get the message.

annotations.meaningVi: có vẻ như thế, tạo ấn tượng như thế
annotations.exampleEn: If I came across as angry, I'm sorry — I didn't mean to.
Small misunderstandings can eat into the trust between colleagues.

annotations.meaningVi: buổi trò chuyện kiểm tra, đánh giá tình hình
annotations.exampleEn: Let's have a quick check-in every Friday to review the week.

annotations.meaningVi: ký được một khách hàng lớn
annotations.exampleEn: If she lands a big client this quarter, she'll need to hire help.

grammarNotes.title: Second Conditional for Hypothetical Proposals
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Hội thoại sử dụng câu điều kiện loại 2 để đề xuất các giải pháp giả định một cách lịch sự: 'What if you paid forty and I paid sixty?', 'What if we book the studio for client meetings?', 'If you have a meeting in the morning, I could just work from a café'. Cấu trúc: 'If + past simple, would/could + verb' (hoặc 'What if + past simple'). Khi đề xuất ý tưởng, dùng dạng giả định (past simple sau if) khiến lời đề nghị nhẹ nhàng và mở để thương lượng.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The dialogue uses second conditional patterns to propose hypothetical solutions politely: 'What if you paid forty and I paid sixty?', 'What if we book the studio for client meetings?', 'If you have a meeting in the morning, I could just work from a café'. Structure: 'If + past simple, would/could + verb' (or 'What if + past simple'). Using the hypothetical form (past simple after if) makes proposals softer and more open to negotiation.

grammarNotes.title: Present Perfect Continuous for Recent, Ongoing Situations
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Người nói dùng thì hiện tại hoàn thành tiếp diễn để mô tả các trạng thái kéo dài và vẫn đang tiếp tục: 'I've been meaning to bring this up for weeks', 'I've been here in the evenings... much more than you have', 'small things were starting to eat into that'. Cấu trúc: have/has + been + V-ing. Khác với hiện tại hoàn thành đơn (nhấn mạnh kết quả), dạng tiếp diễn nhấn mạnh quá trình và sự kéo dài.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Speakers use the present perfect continuous to describe sustained, ongoing situations: 'I've been meaning to bring this up for weeks', 'I've been here in the evenings... much more than you have'. Form: have/has + been + V-ing. Unlike the simple present perfect (which emphasises result), the continuous form emphasises process and duration.

translationVi:
Sam: Andre, mình nói chuyện đàng hoàng về studio được không? Mình đã muốn nói chuyện này nhiều tuần rồi, và nếu không nói, mình nghĩ nó sẽ bắt đầu ảnh hưởng đến công việc.

Andre: Tất nhiên. Mình cũng cảm thấy có gì đó không ổn, thật đấy. Nói đi, bạn đang nghĩ gì.

Sam: Được. Việc chia tiền thuê. Lúc dọn vào mình thoả thuận chia đôi, nhưng mình ở đây buổi tối và cuối tuần nhiều hơn bạn rất nhiều. Cảm giác bắt đầu không công bằng.

Andre: Đúng là vậy. Mình không nhận ra bạn ở đây ngoài giờ thường xuyên thế nào. Bạn muốn chia theo giờ hay đơn giản hơn?

Sam: Mình muốn đơn giản hơn, nếu không tháng nào mình cũng cãi nhau về bảng giờ. Nếu bạn trả bốn mươi còn mình trả sáu mươi thì sao? Như vậy gần với mức sử dụng thực tế hơn.

Andre: Mình có thể chấp nhận, miễn là nó bao gồm tất cả — tiền thuê, điện nước, mực máy in. Mình không muốn bị tính từng đồng lẻ thêm.

Sam: Đồng ý. Sáu mươi-bốn mươi bao toàn bộ chi phí dùng chung. Chỉ chia đều internet thôi, vì cả hai đều dùng dù có ở đây hay không.

Andre: Hợp lý. Điều lớn hơn với mình, thật ra, là không gian gặp khách hàng. Khi bạn có cuộc gặp khách hàng, mình thực sự không làm việc được. Mất nửa ngày mỗi lần.

Sam: Mình biết, và mình xin lỗi. Mình lẽ ra phải hỏi sớm hơn. Nếu mình đặt lịch cho các buổi gặp khách hàng — chỉ một lịch chung thôi — bạn sẽ được báo trước, và mình sẽ kỷ luật hơn.

Andre: Như vậy sẽ giúp rất nhiều. Và nếu bạn có cuộc gặp buổi sáng, mình có thể làm việc ở quán cà phê đến trưa. Chính sự bất ngờ làm mất tập trung, không phải buổi gặp.

Sam: Xong. Tối nay mình sẽ dán lịch trên cửa. Còn gì từ phía bạn đang làm bạn khó chịu không?

Andre: Thật lòng à? Âm nhạc. Mình thích bạn bật nhạc, nhưng có những ngày mình cần yên tĩnh để suy nghĩ, và mình khó nói bạn tắt mà không tỏ ra thô lỗ.

Sam: Ôi, mình không hề biết. Bạn phải nói chứ. Từ giờ, cứ đeo tai nghe vào — đó có thể là tín hiệu rằng bạn cần yên tĩnh. Mình sẽ hiểu ngay.

Andre: Như vậy dễ hơn nhiều việc phải nói thành lời mỗi lần. Cảm ơn bạn.

Sam: Một chuyện nữa khi mình đang nói thẳng. Cú pitch mình mất tháng trước — mình thấy bạn đổ lỗi cho mình một chút, dù cả hai cùng làm.

Andre: Mình không có ý đó. Nếu mình tỏ ra như thế, mình xin lỗi. Mình thất vọng với bản thân mình hơn bất cứ ai, nhưng lẽ ra mình phải nói rõ hơn.

Sam: Cảm ơn vì bạn nói vậy. Mình nghĩ cuộc nói chuyện này lẽ ra phải có từ nhiều tháng trước. Studio hoạt động được vì mình tin nhau, và những chuyện nhỏ đang ăn mòn niềm tin đó.

Andre: Đúng vậy. Mình thoả thuận cứ vài tháng làm thế này — một buổi check-in đàng hoàng, không chỉ là uống cà phê. Sửa chuyện nhỏ tốt hơn đợi một trong hai muốn rời đi.

Sam: Đồng ý. Mình thà thương lượng tiền thuê với bạn còn hơn sáu tháng tìm bạn studio mới.

Andre: Mình cũng vậy. Được — lịch trên cửa, tai nghe để yên tĩnh, sáu mươi-bốn mươi tiền thuê. Còn gì mình quên không?

Sam: Một chuyện nữa. Nếu năm tới một trong hai ký được khách hàng lớn, mình nên bàn xem mở rộng hay giữ nguyên cỡ này. Mình không muốn tự giả định.

Andre: Suy nghĩ hay. Thêm vào lần check-in tới. Bây giờ, mình nghĩ mình xứng đáng được cốc cà phê — và một giờ yên tĩnh để thực sự làm được việc.

questions.prompt: Why does Sam want to talk to Andre?
options: To raise issues that have been building up
To ask Andre to leave the studio
questions.explanation: Sam says 'I've been meaning to bring this up for weeks, and... it's going to start affecting our work.'
questions.hint: Sam describes feelings that have accumulated.

questions.prompt: What was the original rent split when they moved in?
questions.explanation: Sam says 'We agreed fifty-fifty when we moved in.'
questions.hint: Sam mentions the original agreement at the start.

questions.prompt: Why does Sam reject an hours-based split?
options: It would mean arguing about timesheets every month
It is illegal under the lease
questions.explanation: Sam says 'I'd prefer something simpler, otherwise we'll spend every month arguing about timesheets.'
questions.hint: Sam wants to avoid an ongoing source of conflict.

questions.prompt: What new split do they agree on for shared costs?
options: Sixty paid by Sam, forty paid by Andre
questions.explanation: Sam proposes 'What if you paid forty and I paid sixty?' and Andre agrees.
questions.hint: Sam pays more because Sam uses the studio more.

questions.prompt: What is the only cost they still split equally?
questions.explanation: Sam says 'The only thing we'd still split equally is internet, because we both use it whether we're here or not.'
questions.hint: It's a service used regardless of physical presence.

options: Losing half a day each time Sam has a client meeting
questions.explanation: Andre says 'When you have a client meeting, I can't really work. I lose half a day every time.'
questions.hint: Andre's frustration is about productivity loss.

questions.prompt: What solution do they agree on for client meetings?
options: Moving meetings to a coffee shop
Using a shared calendar so Andre has warning
Limiting meetings to one per month
Sam stops taking client meetings entirely
questions.explanation: Sam says 'What if we book the studio for client meetings — even just a shared calendar?'
questions.hint: The solution is a planning tool, not a ban.

questions.prompt: What does Andre say bothers him about Sam's music?
options: The volume is always too loud
Some days he needs silence and finds it hard to ask
questions.explanation: Andre says 'some days I need silence to think, and I find it hard to ask you to turn it off without sounding rude.'
questions.hint: It's about specific days, not the music in general.

questions.prompt: What signal will Andre use when he needs quiet?
questions.explanation: Sam says 'just put on your headphones — that can be the signal that you need quiet.'
questions.hint: The signal is visual, not verbal.

questions.prompt: What does Sam say about the pitch they lost last month?
options: Sam felt Andre had blamed Sam a little
questions.explanation: Sam says 'I felt you blamed me a little, even though we'd both worked on it.'
questions.hint: Sam expresses a feeling about Andre's reaction.

questions.prompt: How does Andre explain his behaviour about the lost pitch?
options: He was disappointed in himself more than anything
questions.explanation: Andre says 'I was disappointed in myself more than anything, but I should have been clearer about that.'
questions.hint: Andre identifies the real source of his disappointment.

questions.prompt: How often do they agree to have check-ins from now on?
questions.explanation: Andre says 'Let's agree to do this every couple of months — a proper check-in.'
questions.hint: The frequency is neither very often nor rare.

questions.prompt: What overall lesson does this conversation illustrate?
options: Studio shares never work long term
Small issues can damage trust if they are not discussed
questions.explanation: Sam says 'small things were starting to eat into' the trust between them — and the whole conversation shows that direct discussion repairs it.
questions.hint: Look at Sam's reflection in the second half.

cloze.template:
Sam: Andre, can we talk properly about the studio? I've been meaning to {{b1}} this up for weeks.
Andre: Of course. I've felt something was off too. Tell me what's on your mind.
Sam: The rent {{b2}}. We agreed fifty-fifty when we moved in, but I've been here in the evenings and weekends much more than you have. It's started to feel {{b3}}.
Andre: That's a fair point. Would you prefer an hours-based split, or something simpler?
Sam: I'd prefer something simpler. What if you paid forty and I paid sixty? That feels closer to actual {{b4}}.
Andre: I could live with that, as long as it covers everything. I don't want to be {{b5}} on top.
Sam: Agreed. The only thing we'd still split equally is internet.
Andre: The bigger thing for me is client space. When you have a client meeting, I lose half a {{b6}} every time.
Sam: I'm sorry. What if we book the studio for client meetings — even just a shared {{b7}}?
Andre: That would help. The {{b8}} is what kills my focus, not the meeting itself.
Sam: Anything from your side that's been bothering you?
Andre: The music. Some days I need silence and I find it hard to ask without sounding {{b9}}.
Sam: From now on, just put on your headphones — that can be the {{b10}} that you need quiet.

blanks.explanation: 'Bring something up' means to mention or raise a topic — the standard phrasal verb in this context.
'Rent split' is the fixed expression for how rent is divided between people sharing a space.
'Uneven' is the exact word Sam uses — it specifically describes an imbalance in shares or usage.
'Usage' is a noun referring to how much something is used — 'actual usage' is the natural collocation.
'Nickel-and-dimed' is an idiom meaning charged repeatedly for small extras — fits the speaker's irritation.
'Half a day' is the exact phrase used to describe the productivity loss.
'Shared calendar' is the standard solution Sam proposes — a digital tool both can see.
Andre says 'It's the surprise that kills my focus' — being caught unprepared is the issue.
'Without sounding rude' is the exact phrase — the issue is politeness, not other qualities.
'Signal' specifically refers to a non-verbal cue that conveys information — fits the use of headphones.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Sam and Andre fix their problems by talking openly. Why is it often easier to live with small frustrations than to bring them up — and what is the right moment to have a conversation like this in a working relationship?
